content.text: The Shared Responsibility Model is not a single fixed boundary — it shifts depending on which AWS service type you choose. The further you move from self-managed infrastructure (IaaS) toward managed platform services (PaaS) and serverless, the more AWS takes on and the less operational security work you perform. The core insight:
your IAM configuration, your application code, and your data classification remain your responsibility regardless of service type.
Everything else progressively shifts to AWS as you move up the abstraction stack.

attrs.alt: Shared responsibility matrix across EC2 (IaaS), RDS/ECS (PaaS), Lambda/S3 (serverless), and SaaS service types

content.text: EC2 is the most exposed service type from a customer-responsibility perspective. You control a virtual machine — guest OS and above — which gives you maximum flexibility but also maximum security responsibility.

Physical servers, racks, power, cooling, network cabling, the Nitro hypervisor, hardware security modules, BIOS/firmware integrity, and the global network backbone. You have no visibility into or control over any of this.

Everything that runs inside the VM: the operating system (patching CVEs, hardening the kernel, managing SSH keys), any middleware (web servers, databases, message queues), your application code, application-layer firewall rules, and IAM configuration for the instance profile.

On EC2, you must harden the guest OS yourself. This includes: disabling unused services and ports, removing default credentials, enforcing SELinux or AppArmor policies, configuring auditd for system call logging, and ensuring sshd is configured to disallow root login and password authentication.

You must track CVEs affecting your OS and installed packages and apply patches in a timely manner. A common mistake: launching an EC2 instance from an AMI that was current six months ago and never updating it. The instance accumulates vulnerabilities silently while running in production.
Why teams choose IaaS despite the overhead:

EC2 gives you root access, the ability to run any software, and complete control over OS configuration. This is necessary for software that cannot run in containers, requires specific kernel modules, uses SR-IOV networking for high performance, or needs bare-metal characteristics (aws EC2 bare metal instances).
PaaS — Platform as a Service (RDS, ECS, Elastic Beanstalk)

PaaS services abstract the OS and sometimes the runtime, shifting OS and platform patching to AWS while you retain responsibility for the application and its configuration. The boundary is less clear than IaaS — it varies by service.
Amazon RDS — The PaaS Database Model

RDS is the canonical PaaS example. AWS manages the EC2 instance, the guest OS, the database engine installation, and the engine patching schedule. You manage the database schema, user accounts, connection configuration, parameter groups, and encryption settings.

Provisioning the DB instance, installing and patching the database engine (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server), applying OS security patches, managing automated backups, and replacing failed hardware.

Database user accounts and privileges (GRANT statements), the schema design, connection pool configuration, parameter group tuning, enabling encryption at rest (must be done at creation), enabling deletion protection, and network controls (which VPC, which security groups, whether to place in private subnet).

RDS applies engine patches during your defined maintenance window. AWS gives advance notice for patches that require a brief reboot. You choose when this window is — pick off-peak hours and use Multi-AZ to minimize downtime (the standby is promoted while the primary reboots).

For RDS, the database engine version is a shared responsibility. AWS applies minor version patches automatically (configurable). Major version upgrades require customer initiation and testing — you choose when to upgrade from MySQL 8.0 to 8.4 and run the pre-upgrade tests.
Amazon ECS — The Shared Container Model
ECS is more complex because the responsibility split depends on launch type.
, you own the underlying EC2 host — its OS patches, Docker daemon version, and storage driver configuration. If a vulnerability is found in the Docker engine, you must update it. With
, AWS manages the host OS, container runtime, and kernel. Your container image runs in an isolated microVM (built on Firecracker). You only manage the image and the task configuration.

Serverless services represent the highest level of managed infrastructure. AWS handles the physical layer, hypervisor, OS, runtime, and scaling. You are responsible for function code, IAM permissions, and event source configuration.

AWS manages the execution environment (the managed runtime for Node.js, Python, Java, etc.), the underlying EC2 host, the OS, memory isolation between concurrent invocations, and automatic scaling. You manage your function code, the execution role (IAM), environment variables (use Secrets Manager references rather than plaintext), and the function's VPC configuration if it needs private resource access.

AWS automatically updates managed runtimes within a supported major version (e.g., Python 3.12.x patches). When a major version reaches end-of-life (e.g., Python 3.8), AWS deprecates it and you must migrate your function. AWS provides several months of advance notice and a grace period before deprecation enforces a migration.

AWS manages the storage hardware, software, replication, and durability mechanisms (the 11 nines of durability comes entirely from AWS-managed infrastructure). You manage: bucket policies (who can read/write), encryption configuration (SSE-S3 is now default; choose SSE-KMS for regulated data), versioning, lifecycle rules, access logging, and Block Public Access settings.

AWS manages everything below the API: hardware, replication across AZs, patching, scaling, and point-in-time recovery mechanics. You manage: table design (partition key selection, GSIs), capacity mode (on-demand vs provisioned), encryption (enabled by default with AWS-owned key; choose CMK for compliance), IAM policies governing which principals can call which operations, and backup configuration.

# Lambda: retrieve secret from Secrets Manager at runtime
# (never store secrets in environment variables)
import boto3, json, os

def get_db_creds():
    client = boto3.client("secretsmanager")
    secret = client.get_secret_value(SecretId=os.environ["SECRET_ARN"])
    return json.loads(secret["SecretString"])

# The execution role must have:
# secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on the specific secret ARN
# No other permissions on secretsmanager
SaaS — Software as a Service (WorkMail, Chime, Connect)

AWS also offers SaaS products — applications delivered over the internet where AWS manages the entire stack except end-user administration. The customer responsibility reduces to user provisioning, data input, and configuration within the SaaS application's admin console.

Everything from physical hardware through the application layer — infrastructure, runtime, application code, updates, availability. You interact only via a web interface or API, not infrastructure controls.

User account management (who has access to the SaaS), configuration of application features, data that users enter into the system, and compliance with any data residency requirements within the application's available options.

Even with SaaS, you remain responsible for access control (who in your organization can log in and with what permissions), data classification (what data you put into the system and whether that's appropriate given the service's compliance certifications), and SSO/federation configuration (connecting the SaaS to your identity provider).
Choosing a Service Type: Security Trade-offs

Security teams often push teams toward PaaS and serverless not just for operational efficiency but because the reduced attack surface makes security posture more maintainable. An unpatched EC2 instance is a ticking clock; an unpatched Lambda function has no OS to patch.
A common misconception: moving from EC2 to Lambda eliminates security responsibility. The reality is that responsibility

rather than disappears. With Lambda, OS and runtime patches vanish from your list — but IAM permission scoping becomes even more critical because a Lambda function's execution role determines exactly what an attacker can do if they exploit your function code.
Serverless IAM is more critical, not less:

With EC2, a compromised process might be contained by OS-level user isolation. With Lambda, the execution role's IAM permissions determine the entire blast radius of a compromise. A Lambda function with AdministratorAccess means a code injection vulnerability in your function gives an attacker full account access.
Application code security becomes the primary risk:

When AWS handles infrastructure, the remaining attack surface is your code. SQL injection, XSS, SSRF, insecure deserialization, and path traversal vulnerabilities don't depend on whether you're on EC2 or Lambda — they exist in your application logic. SAST tools (Semgrep, CodeQL, Snyk Code) and DAST (OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite) remain essential.

Every npm package, PyPI library, or Maven artifact you include is part of your attack surface. A compromised dependency (as in the event-stream npm incident or SolarWinds supply chain attack) can affect serverless functions just as badly as server-based applications — sometimes worse, because the function executes with cloud credentials.
When preparing for a compliance audit or conducting an internal security review, mapping your architecture to the responsibility matrix prevents gaps and avoids duplicating effort.

For each AWS service in your architecture, explicitly state the service type (IaaS/PaaS/serverless) and list what you are responsible for. This becomes evidence for auditors that you have thought through the boundary.

AWS provides pre-filled compliance questionnaires (CAIQ, SIG Lite) and control inheritance matrices for major frameworks (NIST 800-53, ISO 27001, SOC 2). Download these from AWS Artifact and reference them in your audit evidence package — you only need to document your side of the controls.

AWS Config continuous compliance recording provides auditors with a time-series record of your resource configurations. Instead of a point-in-time screenshot, you can show that your S3 buckets had Block Public Access enabled every day for the past 12 months — a far stronger evidence package.

# Generate a compliance evidence report for a specific Config rule
aws configservice get-compliance-details-by-config-rule \
  --config-rule-name s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited \
  --compliance-types COMPLIANT NON_COMPLIANT \
  --query "EvaluationResults[*].{Resource:EvaluationResultIdentifier.EvaluationResultQualifier.ResourceId,Status:ComplianceType,Time:ResultRecordedTime}" \
  --output table
Deep Dive: EC2 Security Controls You Must Implement

Because EC2 is the most open IaaS service, it requires the most comprehensive customer-owned security configuration. This section walks through each control tier that the customer is responsible for on EC2 and why each matters.

An EC2 instance launched from an AWS-provided Amazon Linux 2023 AMI starts in a relatively secure default state, but hardening still requires explicit configuration. Disable root SSH login in sshd_config. Remove unnecessary packages (yum/apt) to reduce the installed software CVE surface. Enable automatic security updates (dnf-automatic on Amazon Linux, unattended-upgrades on Ubuntu). Configure the OS firewall (firewalld or ufw) as a defense-in-depth layer behind your security group. These configurations should be baked into a custom AMI using Packer, not applied manually at instance launch.

The IMDS at 169.254.169.254 provides credentials, instance metadata, and user data to applications running on the instance. IMDSv1 (no authentication) is vulnerable to SSRF — a web application vulnerability that causes the server to make HTTP requests to 169.254.169.254 can steal IAM credentials. Enforce IMDSv2 (which requires a PUT request to get a session token before the GET can retrieve credentials) in your launch template. This eliminates the SSRF credential theft vector entirely.

Replace SSH access with AWS Systems Manager Session Manager for all interactive access to EC2 instances. Session Manager provides shell access through the SSM Agent without opening any inbound ports in security groups, without SSH keys, and with full session logging to CloudWatch Logs or S3. Remove all security group rules allowing port 22 inbound. This eliminates the exposure of SSH to network-based attacks.

Inspector v2 monitors installed software packages on the EC2 instance (via SSM Agent) and correlates them against the National Vulnerability Database (NVD). When a new CVE is published against a package you have installed, Inspector creates a finding immediately — you do not need to run a scan manually or on a schedule. Inspector findings integrate with Security Hub and EventBridge for automated remediation workflows.

Snapshots of unencrypted EBS volumes are also unencrypted. If those snapshots are shared (accidentally or intentionally) with another AWS account, the data is exposed. Enable default EBS encryption at the account level so that all new volumes and all copies of snapshots are encrypted. For existing unencrypted volumes, create an encrypted snapshot copy, then restore from the encrypted snapshot.
RDS removes OS and engine patching from your plate but leaves a substantial set of security controls that are easy to overlook, especially when rapidly deploying for development or testing.

An RDS instance in a public subnet with a publicly accessible endpoint (PubliclyAccessible: true) is reachable from the internet — the only barrier is username/password authentication. Always place RDS instances in private subnets with no route to an internet gateway. Use security groups to allow access only from specific application-tier security groups, not from 0.0.0.0/0.

Beyond username/password, RDS for MySQL and PostgreSQL supports IAM database authentication. Applications authenticate by calling the AWS API to get a short-lived authentication token (valid 15 minutes), then connecting using that token as the password. This eliminates long-lived database passwords and integrates with IAM for access control. Particularly useful for Lambda functions and ECS tasks that already have IAM execution roles.

RDS Enhanced Monitoring provides OS-level metrics (CPU, memory, disk I/O) for the DB instance at up to 1-second granularity. Enable database audit logging (MySQL general log and slow query log; PostgreSQL pgaudit) to CloudWatch Logs. This provides the visibility needed to detect unusual query patterns — such as a SELECT * FROM users query being run by an application role that has never executed it before.

Enable deletion protection on all production RDS instances. This prevents accidental or malicious deletion via the AWS API. Deletion protection must be explicitly disabled before a DROP TABLE or instance deletion can succeed — adding a human confirmation step to an otherwise automatable destruction.

RDS automated backups (1–35 day retention) enable point-in-time recovery. If a ransomware attack corrupts your database or a runaway migration deletes data, you can restore to any second within the retention window. Configure backup retention to 35 days for production databases and verify restore procedures quarterly.

# RDS IAM authentication: generate a temporary auth token
import boto3

rds = boto3.client("rds", region_name="us-east-1")

token = rds.generate_db_auth_token(
    DBHostname="mydb.cluster-xyz.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com",
    Port=5432,
    DBUsername="app_user",
    Region="us-east-1"
)

# Connect with token as password (token expires in 15 minutes)
conn = psycopg2.connect(
    host="mydb.cluster-xyz.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com",
    user="app_user", password=token,
    database="appdb", sslmode="verify-full",
    sslrootcert="/etc/ssl/rds-ca-bundle.pem"
)

Lambda's minimal customer-responsibility surface is deceiving. The responsibility is narrow but each item is high-stakes: IAM execution roles, environment variable handling, dependency security, and event source trust all require careful attention.
One function, one role, minimal permissions:

Every Lambda function should have a dedicated IAM execution role with only the permissions that specific function needs. Do not share execution roles across functions — sharing means a compromise of any function in the group gains access to all permissions the role holds. Use IAM condition keys to restrict which specific resources a function can access (e.g., a specific S3 bucket ARN, not s3:*).

Lambda environment variables are encrypted at rest with an AWS-managed KMS key by default. For sensitive values (tokens, passwords, connection strings), use a customer-managed KMS key for encryption, or better: reference secrets by ARN and retrieve them at runtime using the AWS Parameters and Secrets Lambda Extension, which caches values in memory without making SDK calls on every invocation.

Lambda functions that process events from API Gateway, SQS, SNS, or EventBridge must validate that the event structure and content are trustworthy. API Gateway can enforce IAM authorization, Lambda authorizers, or Cognito JWT validation before invoking your function. For SQS, any principal with SendMessage permission can put messages in the queue — validate message schemas and implement idempotency to handle duplicate deliveries safely.
VPC Lambda for private resource access:

Lambda functions that need to access RDS, ElastiCache, or other VPC resources must be placed in a VPC. VPC Lambda functions use Hyperplane ENIs — managed network interfaces that are reused across invocations without the cold-start penalty of the old per-invocation ENI creation model. Place Lambda functions in private subnets and use a NAT Gateway for any internet access they require.

Lambda layers package shared dependencies across functions. If a shared library in a layer contains a vulnerability, every function using that layer is affected. Pin layer ARNs to specific versions (not :latest) and update layers through your CI/CD pipeline after testing, just like updating a Python package in requirements.txt.
Mapping Common Security Controls to Service Types
Migrating Workloads Up the Service Stack for Security Benefit

One of the most effective security improvement strategies is migrating workloads from IaaS to PaaS or serverless — reducing the customer attack surface without changing the application's business logic. This is sometimes called the "lift-and-shift plus" strategy: after lifting an application to AWS, incrementally replace self-managed components with managed equivalents.
Replace self-managed MySQL on EC2 with RDS:

This single migration eliminates OS-level security responsibilities for the database host: no more CVE tracking for the Linux kernel or MySQL binaries, no more manual backup scripts, no more manual failover procedures during patching. The security team's attention shifts entirely to database-level controls: user privileges, encryption, audit logging.
Replace self-managed Kafka on EC2 with Amazon MSK:

Running Kafka on EC2 requires patching the JVM, the Zookeeper ensemble (or KRaft configuration), and the Kafka binaries themselves. MSK manages all of this. MSK also provides native integration with IAM for authentication (instead of SASL/SCRAM credentials) and TLS encryption in transit between brokers and clients.
Replace cron jobs on EC2 with EventBridge + Lambda:

A cron job on an EC2 instance requires that instance to run 24/7 (cost) and requires OS-level credentials for the user running the cron job. Moving the same logic to a Lambda function invoked by EventBridge Scheduler eliminates the long-running instance, removes the OS-level user account, and allows the function's execution role to be scoped to exactly what the cron job needs — nothing more.
Replace Nginx on EC2 with CloudFront + ALB:

A reverse proxy on EC2 requires patching Nginx, managing TLS certificate renewal, and maintaining the EC2 instance. CloudFront and ALB handle TLS termination, ACM manages certificate renewal automatically, and WAF integrates directly — all without an EC2 instance to patch. The customer's responsibility reduces to WAF rules and ALB configuration.

Security-motivated refactoring doesn't require a complete application rewrite. Replacing infrastructure components one-by-one with managed equivalents progressively narrows the customer attack surface while the application continues serving traffic.

AWS allows customers to perform penetration testing against their own resources without prior approval for most service types. The testing scope and appropriate techniques differ across IaaS, PaaS, and serverless architectures.

You may test EC2 instances you own using standard network and application penetration testing techniques: port scanning, vulnerability scanning (Nessus, OpenVAS), exploitation of identified vulnerabilities, privilege escalation within the instance, and lateral movement simulation. You may not attempt to exploit the hypervisor, escape to other instances, or test AWS's own infrastructure. Port flooding, DDoS simulation, and DNS zone walking require pre-approval.

You may test your RDS database endpoint for application-level vulnerabilities: SQL injection in your application code, brute force of database credentials (at limited rate), testing database parameter group configurations. You may not attempt to exploit the RDS control plane, the underlying EC2 host, or the automated backup system.

Testing Lambda functions focuses on application security: API fuzzing against the API Gateway endpoint, event injection testing (crafting malicious SQS/SNS/S3 events), SSRF testing (attempting to reach IMDS at 169.254.169.254 from function code), and IAM privilege escalation testing (can the execution role be abused to gain additional permissions?). Lambda's serverless nature makes traditional network scanning less relevant.

FIS allows you to run controlled chaos experiments on your AWS resources to test resilience — not security specifically, but resilience is a security property. Simulate EC2 instance failures, AZ outages, network packet loss, and API error injection to verify that your architecture degrades gracefully rather than failing catastrophically.
The boundary shifts with service type:

EC2 (IaaS) leaves OS, runtime, and middleware to you. RDS/ECS (PaaS) takes on OS and engine patching. Lambda/S3 (serverless) takes everything except your code and IAM. SaaS takes the application itself — only user access and data remain yours.
Three things never shift to AWS:

Your IAM configuration, your application code security, and your data classification and encryption choices remain customer responsibilities regardless of service type. These are the areas to concentrate your security investment.

Serverless reduces operational security burden (no OS patches) but concentrates risk in IAM and code. A Lambda function with excessive IAM permissions and a code injection vulnerability is more dangerous than an EC2 instance running the same code with OS-level mitigations.
Use service type selection as a security lever:

When a team asks whether to use EC2 or Fargate for a workload, the security answer is almost always Fargate — it eliminates the EC2 host patching burden with no security downside. Reserve EC2 for workloads that genuinely require it.

For each managed or serverless service you use, the physical, hypervisor, and OS controls are pre-audited by AWS. Download the relevant certification from AWS Artifact and reference the control inheritance map — this eliminates a large block of audit work.
